Most people think quants predict market direction. We usually donât

Hereâs what they do predict: volatility, how much prices move.

Key points:

Volatility clusters: big moves follow big moves, small moves follow small moves.

GARCH(1,1) captures it with three terms:

baseline (long-term variance)

shock (recent squared return)

memory (previous variance)

Persistence is high: Î± + Î² â 0.9 - 0.99 -> volatility can stay elevated for weeks or months.

Constant-vol models fail: they underestimate risk after shocks

VaR illustrates the impact: same portfolio, same confidence level, but risk estimates can change dramatically

Takeaway: predicting risk, not returns, is where quants find a signal

$VIX hits 30 â Buy stocks
$VIX hits 45+ â Buy even more
$VIX hits 14 â Sell / trim

Repeat endlessly.

Hereâs why it works.

The VIX is the Fear Index.

When it spikes, institutions are panic selling.
Prices are at maximum discount.
Thatâs when you want to be buying â not selling.

VIX above 30 = fear is elevated = opportunity
VIX above 45 = capitulation = generational entry points

Every single VIX spike in history â 2008, 2011, 2020, 2022, Liberation Day 2025 â was followed by a recovery.

Every. Single. One.

And when VIX hits 14?

Nobody is scared.
Complacency is at its peak.
Thatâs when smart money quietly trims and prepares for the next cycle.

Buy fear.
Sell greed.
Repeat endlessly.

The rule is simple because it works.

When firms like Andreessen Horowitz and Y Combinator begin openly sharing startup ideas, founders should pay attention. These are two of the most influential organizations in venture capital, and both are signaling where they believe the next wave of billion-dollar companies will come from in 2026.

The message is very clear in the eyes and wallets of both VCâs. AI is moving beyond chatbots and productivity tools. They are pointing toward AI that enters the physical world, replaces entire service businesses, and rebuilds software for agents instead of humans. Y Combinator is highlighting broken industries like farming, legal services, and outdated chip infrastructure. Andreessen Horowitz is pointing to solo founders powered by AI CFOs, autonomous sales engines, and digital chiefs of staff that operate nonstop. What makes this moment different is the precision. I remember when headlines like this would immediately create a sense of urgency and fear. The idea of a market crash can make anyone feel like they need to act quickly, but over time I realized that reacting emotionally is rarely the best strategy.

Markets move in cycles, and predictions are made constantly. Some turn out to be right, many do not. What matters more is having a plan that does not depend on short term news or sudden reactions.

Experienced investors focus on risk management, diversification, and long term thinking. Instead of trying to predict every move, they prepare for different outcomes and stay consistent with their strategy.

The real lesson is not to panic but to stay informed and disciplined. When you understand how markets work and manage risk wisely, you put yourself in a stronger position regardless of what happens next.

Comment âClaudeâ to get the link.

Most people run out of tokens early because of how they use Claude, not how much they use it. 

Every message carries past context, so longer chats slowly become expensive.

A simple shift helps: give cleaner inputs. 

Remove extra words, avoid repeating context, and be specific. 

This reduces how much Claude has to process each time.

Also, Claude doesnât need your full data every time. 

If you share only the important parts (like summaries or key points), it performs the same task with fewer tokens.

Model choice also matters. Using a heavier model for simple tasks increases token usage without adding real value.

Think of it like this: the clearer and shorter your input, the less work Claude has to do. And the longer your session lasts.

I have shared a lot more in the guide. Comment "Claude" and I'll share it with you.

If you want to work in quant trading, these are the Python projects students should build to understand how markets actually behave.

1. Monte Carlo market simulator
This is one of the first models quants build. Simulate thousands of possible price paths using geometric Brownian motion to understand how volatility and randomness shape outcomes. Instead of predicting a single price, the simulation produces a distribution of possible futures. From that distribution you can estimate probabilities, downside risk, and confidence intervals. This project teaches the mindset shift from prediction to probability.

2. Strategy backtesting engine
A backtester allows you to test trading ideas on historical data. The core pipeline should include data ingestion, signal generation, position sizing, transaction costs, and performance metrics. Many beginners focus only on returns, but the real lesson is understanding how assumptions break strategies when costs, slippage, or volatility regimes change.

3. Volatility targeting model
Many quantitative funds scale position sizes based on volatility. A volatility targeting project estimates rolling volatility and adjusts exposure to maintain a consistent risk level. This teaches how risk is managed dynamically and why raw returns without volatility control are misleading.

4. Factor model analysis
Use Python to study how assets move relative to common risk factors such as market, value, or momentum. This project introduces regression, covariance matrices, and factor exposure. It also shows how diversification actually depends on correlation structure rather than simply holding more assets.

5. Order book or microstructure simulator
Even a simplified order book model reveals how liquidity and order flow affect prices. By simulating bids, asks, and trade executions, you can explore how spreads, inventory risk, and liquidity shape trading behaviour.

Quant trading isnât about guessing markets.
Itâs about building models that reveal how they behave.

Save this if youâre learning quant trading from zero.
Follow for more quant foundations.

What youâre looking at is an observable Markov regime engine running over a live state stream. Instead of guessing direction, the model classifies the market into four structural regimes:

calm trend
volatile trend
chop
risk-off

Each bar is converted into a probabilistic state using a weighted combination of volatility, trend strength, drawdown pressure, correlation stress, and shock intensity.

From there, the system doesnât just classify. It builds a rolling transition structure:

P(next state | current state)

That means the model is constantly learning how regimes evolve, persist, and break.

But raw classification is noisy. So the engine applies temporal stabilizers:

Hysteresis to prevent weak flips
Minimum persistence windows to enforce structure
Majority bias to reduce single-bar noise

And most importantly, risk conditioning:

Risk-off states require confirmed stress
Overextended regimes get rebalanced
Chop dominance is actively suppressed

What you see visually is not random animation.
Itâs a phase-space projection of the system:

Regime clusters act as attractors
Trajectory shows how the market is drifting
Vector fields show where itâs likely to transition next

This is how you stop reacting to priceâ¦
and start tracking the system underneath it.

If you understand the transitions, you understand the market. Get the model free at edgebuildit.com #trading #investing #AI #quantfinance #finance

â³ Context is the new competitive advantage.

 Most people open Claude and start prompting from scratch every single time. 

They lose everything from the last session, the decisions they made, the architecture they chose, the bugs they already fixed.

 Me and @speedy_devv built a system where Obsidian holds every decision, every conversation, every lesson learned, and Claude reads it all before writing a single line of code. 

The best part is that the agents we run donât just know what to build, they know WHY weâre building it and what weâve already tried.

The result is that our agents get smarter every single day we use them. 

Not because the model improved, but because the context did.

With the recent Claude updates from the last couple of weeks this has gotten incredibly powerful. 

Your AI is only as good as what it knows about your project, and most people are feeding it almost nothing.

This context system facilitates one of the core parts of the orchestration framework me and @speedy_devv built for ourselves over the past two years. 

Itâs been really cool to see other builders start using it and setting up their own context vaults alongside it.

You can find the full framework at buildthisnow.com, link in bio.

The arrival of a baby Pallasâs cat at the Helsinki Zoo has completely taken the internet by storm, and for good reason. These elusive wild cats, also known as manuls, are famous for their expressive faces and incredibly dense, fluffy fur, but seeing one in its infancy is a rare treat that most wildlife enthusiasts canât resist. The images shared from the zoo show a tiny, wide-eyed kitten being carefully held by a keeper, highlighting just how small and precious these creatures are at the start of their lives. Native to the cold, rocky steppes of Central Asia, Pallasâs cats are built for extreme environments, possessing the thickest fur of any cat species to help them survive freezing temperatures. Despite their somewhat grumpy-looking adult reputation, this kitten embodies pure innocence, capturing hearts globally with its round ears and intense gaze. This birth is not just a win for "cuteness" seekers but also a significant moment for conservation efforts and zoological milestones, as breeding these specialized felines in captivity requires immense care and expertise. It is a gentle reminder of the diverse beauty found in the animal kingdom and why protecting these unique species is so vital.

Every day, thousands of oil tankers and cargo ships move across the oceans long before markets react.

Energy traders track this movement using satellite imagery and live vessel signals that show exactly where ships are loading, unloading, or waiting offshore. Platforms like the Bloomberg Terminal map more than 320,000 data fields in real time, combining satellite images with terrestrial AIS vessel feeds. By monitoring oil tankers, LNG carriers, and container ships, analysts can estimate physical supply flows days or even weeks before official reports. Since commodities move physically before prices adjust, these shipping patterns often provide early clues about supply shortages, surpluses, and potential market shifts.

Sometimes the most important signal in the market is not a price chart, but a ship quietly changing course.

#OilMarkets #Commodities #BloombergTerminal #EnergyTrading #TradingNews
